_meta.note: BuzzFeed quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked · answer-quiz (first option correct)

title_001: How Change Happens — Dopamine Master Quiz
html_005: The science of complex contagion & the 25% tipping point
html_008: One stranger’s word isn’t enough — only when several people around you move do you finally move too.”
html_009: — Damon Centola, Change: How to Make Big Things Happen
html_010: Why does information spread so easily while behavior doesn’t? You’ll lock in the heart of network science through
html_012:
.
        You start easy and go deeper, and
html_013: the ones you miss keep coming back until they click.
html_030: That’s the end of your free preview
html_043: How Change Happens at a glance
html_046: and you’ll never forget it. It all starts with
html_047: knowing how information differs from behavior
html_051: It travels on a single weak tie, like news or a virus
html_054: New behavior and belief need many rounds of social confirmation
html_057: Multiple contacts through overlapping acquaintances build trust
html_060: Once a committed minority hits critical mass, the balance tips
html_063: “How Change Happens — the big change that connection makes”
html_069: Focused review of what you missed
html_071: the YouTube ‘How Change Happens’ book-preview video
html_072:
and revisit the core concepts.
      Damon Centola’s book
html_080: A repeat master quiz that turns a book’s core into dopamine
html_081: This app is secondary study content that reworks the book’s core ideas for learning. For the full reading experience, meet the original book.
html_082: How Change Happens · Buy the book

js_004: Who wrote this book, and what’s its original English title?
js_005: Damon Centola / Change: How to Make Big Things Happen
js_006: Malcolm Gladwell / The Tipping Point: How Little Things Can Make a Big Difference
js_009: Damon Centola is a professor at the University of Pennsylvania’s Annenberg School and director of the Network Dynamics Group; he published Change: How to Make Big Things Happen in 2021.
js_011: Which sentence best captures this book’s core message?
js_012: Information can spread through a single contact, but changing behavior needs many repeated social confirmations.
js_013: Every human acts only on their inborn nature and can never, ever be changed at all.
js_014: Social change is always dictated, from the top down, by a small elite few and no one else.
js_015: Change happens completely at random, so any attempt to design it is entirely meaningless.
js_016: This book’s compass is that ‘information and behavior spread in different ways.’ News and viruses travel on a single contact (simple contagion), but new behavior and belief only take hold after repeated confirmation from many people (complex contagion).
js_019: A spread that travels on a single contact, like information or disease.
js_020: A special kind of spread that only travels after being confirmed over and over by many different people, many separate times.
js_021: A kind of spread that travels only through strong, tightly bonded relationships and no other way.
js_022: A kind of spread that travels only after an official endorsement from a panel of experts.
js_023: Simple contagion, like a cold virus or a news headline, jumps from just one contact with a stranger. It spreads fast, but rarely carries all the way to a change in behavior.
js_026: New behavior or belief spreads only after repeated confirmation from several social contacts.
js_027: A phenomenon where the more complex and difficult a piece of information is, the faster it ends up spreading.
js_028: A large-scale international epidemic that erupts in many countries at nearly the same time and spreads across national borders.
js_029: A kind of spread that happens only in a closed, secretive way inside a small group of experts.
js_030: Complex contagion, like exercise, voting, or health habits, isn’t something you buy from one stranger’s word — you move only when several people around you are already in it. It’s the core idea running through the whole book.
js_032: What is the ‘25% tipping point’ this book uncovered?
js_033: Once a committed minority reaches roughly 25% of the whole group, its social conventions can flip over remarkably fast.
js_034: If 25% of the population is opposed, then no change of any kind can ever possibly happen at all.
js_035: A new product has to capture 25% of the whole market before it can ever break even.
js_036: Reorganizations should always be carried out in 25% chunks, one quarter at a time.
js_037: In Centola’s team’s lab experiments, the moment people committed to a new norm reached about 25% of the group, the rest switched over fast. It’s the book’s most famous finding.
js_040: A connection between two groups that share many overlapping mutual acquaintances and are linked through multiple points of contact.
js_041: A link between two groups made through just one single stranger and nothing more at all.
js_042: A massive transport network that links two far-apart cities by high-speed rail or air routes.
js_043: The account of one single influencer who happens to have an enormous number of followers.
js_044: Unlike a narrow bridge (a single weak tie), a wide bridge is a channel where many people overlap between two groups. For behavior to spread, you need this kind of multi-contact bridge.
js_046: What does Centola call the ‘limit of influencers’?
js_047: They’re strong at spreading news, but weaker at changing behavior than we assume.
js_048: Influencers play no role whatsoever — none at all — in either spreading information or changing how people behave and think.
js_049: The more influencers there are, the sooner the tipping point always arrives, without exception.
js_050: Influencers hold sway only over the selling of advertised products, and nothing else.
js_051: One celebrity is great at ‘getting the word out,’ but changing people’s behavior is another matter. Behavior change tends to start not with influencers but at the ordinary periphery of the network.
js_053: What does Centola call the real key to social change?
js_054: People respond less to facts than to the social proof that others already do it.
js_055: People always decide how to act using nothing at all but statistics and factual information.
js_056: Social norms have absolutely no effect whatsoever on an individual’s change in behavior.
js_057: The more information there is, the faster behavior changes, with no exceptions at all.
js_058: Centola says the key to successful social change lies in norms, not information. The sense that ‘everyone’s already doing it’ (social proof) is more powerful than new facts.
js_060: What does this book newly reveal about ‘weak ties’?
js_061: Weak ties, strong for spreading information, can actually hinder the spread of behavior.
js_062: Weak ties are of absolutely no help whatsoever to any kind of spread at all, whether of information or of behavior.
js_063: Weak ties are always more trustworthy than strong ties, in every single case without exception.
js_064: The more weak ties there are, the sooner the tipping point always arrives, no matter what.
js_065: Granovetter’s ‘strength of weak ties’ is powerful for spreading information and job leads, but Centola shows by experiment that for the spread of risky or unfamiliar behavior, a single weak tie isn’t enough.
js_067: How is Change: How to Make Big Things Happen structured?
js_068: Part 1 busts old myths; Part 2 lays out how to design change.
js_069: The entire book is built out of just one single case study and absolutely nothing else.
js_070: Part 1 covers the past, Part 2 the present, and Part 3 the future, strictly in that order.
js_071: It has no theory from the author at all — the whole thing is nothing but interview transcripts with famous people.
js_072: The book splits in two. Part 1 (Ch. 1–3) busts the influencer myth, the virality myth, and the stickiness myth; Part 2 (Ch. 4–8) runs from discovering complex contagion to a hands-on playbook for building wide bridges.
js_076: ppss · The 25% who change the world
js_081: Centola has advised organizations from Apple and Microsoft to the U.S. Army on network-diffusion strategy.
js_082: The book’s original title is ‘Change: How to Make Big Things Happen’ (2021), published in the U.S. that year.
js_083: Simple contagion — colds, rumors, news — jumps on a single contact. It spreads fast but rarely turns into behavior.
js_084: Complex contagion is a spread that crosses its threshold only after social reinforcement stacks up many times over.
js_085: In the lab, the instant a committed minority passed 25%, the group’s majority moved to the new norm in a short time.
js_086: A wide bridge spreads behavior far more powerfully than a narrow one (a single weak tie). It was the core mechanism behind BLM’s spread.
js_087: Influencers have wide reach, but they struggle to generate the repeated social confirmation that behavior change requires.
js_088: The social proof of ‘everyone’s already doing it’ drives behavior far more powerfully than statistics or facts.
js_089: Granovetter’s weak-tie theory is powerful for spreading information, but it has limits for the spread of risky behavior.
js_090: Part 1 busts the old myths; Part 2 lays out the playbook for actually designing change.
js_091: Chapter 1, called ‘the Oprah error,’ pushes back on the belief that overrates the influence of celebrities and media.
js_092: They built several online groups of around 20 people, tuning the share of the committed minority to watch the tipping point.
js_093: Below 25%, the majority kept the old norm no matter how much time passed — the power of critical mass.
js_094: Both the equals-sign campaign and the Ice Bucket Challenge spread explosively once people directly saw close friends take part.
js_095: After George Floyd’s death, BLM spread with unmatched speed thanks to the wide bridges of an already-existing activist network.
js_096: Correlated ties (where your friends are friends with each other) just reconfirm the same opinions and block new information from flowing in.
js_097: Kanter’s critical-mass hypothesis holds that once a minority passes the 20–35% band, an organization’s culture genuinely changes.
js_098: Rather than celebrities at the network’s center, ordinary people at the ‘edges’ — spanning several groups — are often where real spread begins.
js_099: The riskier or costlier the behavior, the more social confirmation has to stack up before it can cross the tipping point.
js_100: When bots generate human-like, repeated social signals, they can be used to promote complex contagion.
js_101: Granovetter (information, job-hunting) and Centola (risky behavior) are complementary theories explaining different domains.
js_102: The 2007 paper ‘Complex Contagions and the Weakness of Long Ties’ is the theoretical root of this book.
js_103: Rather than generalizing controlled online-experiment results straight to the real world, we need the balance of understanding and applying the principle.
js_104: A change-maker can deliberately design wide bridges by connecting people who span several groups.
js_105: Only when early adopters are shielded from early backlash can a committed minority safely grow to 25%.
js_106: Chapter 8 borrows Kuhn’s idea of scientific paradigm shifts to explain how a social norm becomes ‘taken for granted.’
js_107: Focusing on network design can leave structural factors like power and resource inequality relatively underexamined.
js_108: ‘Getting the word out’ favors wide reach (simple contagion); ‘changing behavior’ favors dense, repeated confirmation (complex contagion).
js_109: The craft of designing complex contagion can serve the public good or manipulate opinion, so it calls for ethical care.
js_110: Big change comes not from one person’s charisma, but when wide bridges and repeated confirmation among ordinary people cross 25%.
js_124: Information and viruses spread on a single contact, but new behavior and belief need many rounds of social confirmation.
js_126: One narrow shortcut between groups is weak; a wide bridge — many overlapping acquaintances — carries behavior far better.
js_128: Weak ties that are powerful for spreading information can actually get in the way of spreading risky behavior.
js_129: The moment a committed minority reaches about 25% of a group, social conventions flip fast.
js_130: Celebrities are strong at getting the word out, weak at changing behavior. Real change starts at the edges of the network.
js_132: When only similar people connect, the same information just circles — the network root of the echo-chamber effect.
js_134: People respond less to facts than to the social proof that ‘everyone around me is already doing it.’
js_135: The limit: risky behavior is slow
js_136: The more social confirmation a behavior needs, the slower it spreads — which is why change takes design, not a quick fix.
js_139: Ch. 1: Special people vs. special places — the Arab Spring spread not through celebrities or social-media stars, but through long-built local community ties.
js_140: Ch. 2: Behavior doesn’t spread like a virus — information travels on one contact, but new behavior needs repeated social confirmation.
js_141: Ch. 3: Why great innovations fail — even the best idea won’t spread on ‘stickiness’ alone; that myth gets busted here.
js_142: Key message: celebrities, viral content, and perfect ideas aren’t enough for change. The real power is in network structure.
js_145: Ch. 4: How change happens — in online groups of about 20, the team dialed the share of committed people up and down to find the tipping point.
js_146: The 25% discovery — the moment the committed reach about 25% of the group, the rest flip to the new norm in short order.
js_147: Failure below 25% — when the committed minority falls short of critical mass, change never comes, no matter how long you wait.
js_148: Key: this is where ‘complex contagion,’ the theory behind the whole book, is first proven by experiment.
js_151: The equals-sign campaign — the red equals-sign profile picture for marriage equality exploded among people who saw close friends join in.
js_152: The Ice Bucket Challenge — you only jumped in after watching a friend dump the ice water themselves: complex contagion in its purest form.
js_153: Bots for social good — social bots repeatedly surfaced the same signal, mimicking human social reinforcement in the experiments.
js_154: Key: real virality comes not from fun content, but from the density of relationships that keep confirming it.
js_157: Why wide bridges matter — the more acquaintances two groups share, the wider the bridge, the better behavior crosses it.
js_158: Gaps in the network — the person who fills the empty space between different groups becomes, surprisingly, the architect of change.
js_159: The open-innovation era — lower the walls inside and outside an organization and you widen the channels for new ideas to flow in.
js_160: The Ferguson uprising — thanks to an already-built wide bridge of activist networks, a local protest became a national movement almost overnight.
js_161: Key: change-makers don’t wait for wide bridges — they design them on purpose.
js_163: In search of the new normal (Ch. 8)
js_164: The power of the witch hunt — even a wrong norm can rule a whole group once it gathers repeated social confirmation: a historical warning.
js_165: The Copernican paradigm shift — like scientific common sense being overturned, social norms become ‘obvious’ only after doubt piles up and crosses critical mass.
js_166: Wittgenstein goes to kindergarten — even language and rules gain meaning only through socially agreed confirmation; the book borrows that insight.
js_167: Key: the new normal doesn’t arrive overnight. The moment wide bridges and repeated confirmation cross 25%, it becomes common sense.
js_169: Centola’s team built several online groups of about 20 people, adjusting the share committed to a new name (norm) to watch when the majority would flip. Below 25% it failed; the moment it passed 25%, the whole group moved to the new norm within days.
js_170: It’s the book’s strongest evidence: a number confirmed by a real experiment, not just statistical theory.
js_171: The equals-sign campaign & the Ice Bucket Challenge
js_172: The red equals-sign (=) profile picture for marriage equality and the Ice Bucket Challenge for ALS both spread in a flash. But the power came not from the content itself — it came from repeatedly watching close friends take part.
js_173: It shows the real fuel of spread isn’t a single ‘like’ — it’s the confirmation that ‘several people around me are in it together.’
js_174: George Floyd and the spread of BLM
js_175: Behind the unprecedented speed at which protests went national after George Floyd’s death lay a wide-bridge network that had long overlapped activist communities across many cities. It wasn’t newly built connections but an already-existing dense web of relationships that made the explosive spread possible.
js_176: It shows that what delivers in a crisis isn’t spur-of-the-moment virality, but the infrastructure of relationships laid down in advance.
js_177: A new habit or belief that recently spread near you — did it spread as ‘information,’ or through ‘repeated confirmation’?
js_178: Ask whether one person’s word was enough, or whether it took several people together before you believed it — that’s how you tell simple from complex contagion.
js_179: If there’s a habit in your organization or community you want to change, how could you build a 25% committed group inside it, linked by wide bridges?
js_180: Protect the minority from early backlash, and deliberately connect people across different groups — that’s the starting point.
js_181: Are you leaning too hard on a single influencer right now? Where is the edge of the network that could actually create change?
js_182: Remember: it’s not flashy spread but dense repetition among ordinary people that creates real behavior change.
js_183: You’ve nailed Centola’s core theory cold
js_185: You’ve got almost the whole mechanism of complex contagion
js_187: You’ve got the skeleton — one more round!
js_189: The more you repeat, the deeper it sticks
